taxonomy.label: Code Review & Quality
taxonomy.description: Reviewing diffs, finding bugs, refactoring, enforcing standards.

taxonomy.label: Security & Audits
taxonomy.description: Vulnerability assessment, threat modeling, pentest, trust boundaries.

taxonomy.label: Build & Prototyping
taxonomy.description: From idea to working app, UI generation, feature scaffolding.

taxonomy.label: Content & Writing
taxonomy.description: Blogs, marketing, humanizing text, CVs, docs.

taxonomy.label: Project Intelligence
taxonomy.description: Data extraction, dashboards, project radar, reporting.

taxonomy.label: Dev Workflow & Automation
taxonomy.description: Loops, browser automation, orchestration, environment setup.

taxonomy.label: Integrations & CRM
taxonomy.description: External APIs, CRM, content platforms, connectors.

taxonomy.label: Meta & Skill Tooling
taxonomy.description: Skills about skills: protocols, trust, tooling.

skills.name: cv-master
skills.tagline: Edits, translates, restyles or generates a CV/resume from a PDF, DOCX, link or raw data — outputs editable HTML + a verified PDF.
skills.verdict: Clean security surface: bundled scripts are read-only, and the one network call is a status-only ping with no data sent. Well-built overall. Weak spot: no version field or changelog, so you can't tell what changed between updates.

highlights.summary: No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes in SKILL.md or README.
Install hints (e.g. pip install pillow) are well-known default-registry packages but unpinned; user-run only.
Bundled scripts are read-only (poppler PDF triage, tool probes); the only network call is a status-code-only ping to fonts.googleapis.com with no data sent — verified safe.

skills.name: self-improving-agent
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (3.9k★): captures learnings, errors and corrections so the agent improves over time.
skills.verdict: Trustworthy on security — it writes learnings to its own workspace for its stated purpose, not exfiltration. Docs have a small internal slip (hyphenated format vs underscored examples) and the main file runs a bit long, but nothing that misleads you.

highlights.summary: The stated Pattern-Key format says hyphenated, but the doc's own examples use underscores — a self-inconsistency.
588-line SKILL.md is over the ~500-line budget (still under 2×).
Writes learnings to workspace files for its declared purpose — not secret exfiltration (a weak evaluator over-flags this).

skills.name: skill-vetter
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (1.3k★): a security-first checklist for vetting other skills before install. Meta — evaluated by a grader.
skills.verdict: Security-clean and its listed attack patterns are documentation, not danger. Its blind spot: it never says when NOT to use it, so it could misfire on plain code review instead of security vetting. Also assumes jq is installed without checking.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance — could misfire on general code review.
jq is invoked with no requirements note or availability check.
Lists attack patterns (curl|bash, ~/.ssh, base64) as things to DETECT — correctly read as detection material, not an attack; layer-1 flags 3, the rubric de-escalates.

skills.name: ontology
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (650★): a typed knowledge graph for structured agent memory and composable recall.
skills.verdict: Middling trust. It never states what it's out of scope for despite casting a wide net, so it can trigger on tasks it wasn't built for. It also lacks any version history, and it calls python3 and a script without checking they exist.

highlights.summary: No out-of-scope guidance despite broad triggers — high misfire risk.
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes.
python3 and a bundled script are invoked with no requirements note or availability check.

skills.name: humanizer
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (700★): strips AI-generated tells from text to give writing a more natural human voice.
skills.verdict: Clean text-only transformation — no network calls, no secret access, no code execution anywhere. Its only weakness is not saying when NOT to use it, so it could get invoked on writing tasks it wasn't meant for.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance — could misfire on adjacent writing tasks.
Pure text transformation — no network, secret access, or code execution anywhere (security is n/a or pass throughout).

skills.name: gog
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (941★): a CLI for Google Workspace — Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Contacts, Sheets and Docs.
skills.verdict: Handles your own OAuth token locally and only talks to Google's APIs — no secret exfiltration. But it has no failure handling: if the binary is missing, you're unauthenticated, or a token expired, nothing tells you what to do, and it installs from an unpinned personal Homebrew tap.

highlights.summary: OAuth setup and every networked command can fail (missing binary, unauthenticated, expired token) with no failure handling.
Installs from an unpinned personal Homebrew tap (steipete/tap/gogcli).
Handles the user's own OAuth token locally and talks only to Google APIs — no secret exfiltration, harvesting, or unsolicited egress.

skills.name: weather
skills.tagline: Popular ClawHub skill (423★): current weather and forecasts with no API key required.
skills.verdict: Solid trust: it fetches public weather data only, no secrets touched, nothing leaves your machine. Docs don't say when it shouldn't be used (e.g. historical climate data), and there's no version field to track changes.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance (e.g. historical climate data).
No version field, changelog, or dated revision notes.
Fetches public weather data only — no secrets, no local data sent anywhere. Security clean.

skills.name: imap-smtp-email
skills.tagline: ClawHub skill (107★): reads and sends mail over IMAP/SMTP — checks new mail, fetches and sends messages.
skills.verdict: Falls back to a plaintext password file in its own directory (600 permissions, app-passwords recommended) that never leaves your machine — a real but contained weakness. It also tells you to disable TLS certificate verification for self-signed certs, and its own docs disagree on which config file loads first.

highlights.summary: Config-precedence contradiction between lines 36 and 63 (which .env is checked first).
Advises *_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=false for self-signed certs — weakens TLS verification.
Falls back to a plaintext-password .env in the skill dir (600-perm, app-passwords recommended) — credentials stay local and are never echoed or sent anywhere but the user's own servers.

skills.verdict: Solid dev-tool skill: clear setup steps for every framework, explicit output contract, good fallback/troubleshooting coverage, and worked examples for every required format. Docked for a very long run-on description, unpinned installs of well-known packages, no version/changelog signal, and no stated boundary against the sibling migrate/refactor skills it.

highlights.summary: `npm install @chakra-ui/react @emotion/react` (line 56) and other package managers install well-known packages with no version pin.
No 'NOT for' or exclusion text anywhere; overlaps plausibly with chakra-ui-migrate and chakra-ui-refactor with no stated boundary.
description (lines 3-16) is ~988 chars, a single dense run-on paragraph of trigger phrases, well past sensible sizing.

skills.name: chakra-ui-migrate
skills.tagline: Guides a step-by-step Chakra UI v2-to-v3 migration: packages, codemod, provider, color mode, prop renames, and compound components.
skills.verdict: Strong, safety-conscious migration guide: dry-run-first codemod, commit-before-manual-edits, a validation checklist, and a pointer to a deeper theming skill when scope grows. No security concerns found. Only real gaps are cosmetic: installs of well-known packages aren't version-pinned, and there's no version/changelog signal on the skill itself.

highlights.summary: `npm install @chakra-ui/react @emotion/react` (line 71) and equivalents install well-known packages with no version pin.
No version field, changelog section, or dated revision notes anywhere in the skill.

skills.name: chakra-ui-refactor
skills.tagline: Reviews, critiques, and refactors/converts UI code (HTML, Tailwind, CSS Modules, styled-components, old Chakra) into idiomatic Chakra UI v3.
skills.verdict: Clean, low-risk review/refactor skill: no commands run, no network or secret handling, clear severity-tiered review format and a defined refactor output format with worked before/after conversion tables for every source type. Weak spots are packaging-only: no exclusion text against the overlapping builder/migrate skills, and no version or changelog signal.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' or exclusion text; plausibly overlaps chakra-ui-builder (new components) and chakra-ui-migrate (v2->v3) with no stated boundary.
No version field, changelog section, or dated revision notes anywhere in the skill.

skills.name: actions-and-utilities
skills.tagline: Reference for Phaser 4's Actions namespace and Array/Object/String utility functions for batch game-object operations.
skills.verdict: Accurate, well-structured API reference with concrete signatures and runnable examples; no security issues found. Weak on hygiene: no negative-trigger guidance to prevent misfiring against sibling Phaser skills, and no version/changelog signal to track content changes.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / exclusion guidance anywhere, despite explicit related sibling skills (groups-and-containers, sprites-and-images) that could misfire.
No version field, changelog section, or dated revision notes found anywhere in the file.

skills.name: animations
skills.tagline: Reference for Phaser 4 sprite animation APIs: AnimationManager, AnimationState, spritesheets, atlases, and events.
skills.verdict: Thorough, accurate animation API reference with clear examples and explicit priority rules (e.g. frameRate vs duration). No security concerns. The 482-line file is dense with several large inline reference tables, has no negative-trigger scope guidance, and carries no version/changelog signal.

482-line file inlines several exhaustive reference tables (Configuration Reference, Events, API Quick Reference) rather than splitting bulk material to on-demand references.
No version field, changelog section, or dated revision notes found anywhere in the file.

skills.name: ppt-master
skills.tagline: Routes PPTX generation, templating, filling, and enhancement workflows through gated sub-procedures.
skills.verdict: Well-structured router: explicit blocking gates, deterministic routing, and failure-recovery guidance; no security issues found in this file. It depends on external workflow/reference files not included in this content, so full output-contract and reference-resolution checks are unverifiable. Lacks negative-trigger scope and a versioning signal.

highlights.summary: No 'NOT for' / out-of-scope guidance anywhere despite the skill covering four distinct routes.
Router defers the concrete deliverable format to child route files (e.g. mentions design_spec.md headings) without stating the contract itself here.
References workflows/routing.md, generate-pptx.md, create-template.md, template-fill-pptx.md, native-enhance-pptx.md, and references/<filename>.md, none of which were available to verify existence.
